questions.question: Explain the difference between analog and digital signals with examples.
keyPoints: Analog signals are continuous and can take any value within a range
Digital signals are discrete with finite set of values (usually binary)
Examples: Analog - voice, music, temperature; Digital - computer data, digital TV

questions.question: State and explain Nyquist Theorem for a noiseless channel.
keyPoints: Maximum data rate = 2B × log₂M bits/second
B = bandwidth in Hz
M = number of discrete signal levels
For binary signals (M=2), data rate = 2B bps

questions.question: What is attenuation? How can it be overcome in data transmission?
keyPoints: Attenuation is the loss of signal strength during transmission
Measured in decibels (dB)
Overcome by using: amplifiers, repeaters, proper shielding, appropriate transmission medium

questions.question: Differentiate between bit rate and baud rate with an example.
keyPoints: Bit rate = number of bits transmitted per second
Baud rate = number of signal changes per second
Example: If each signal change represents 4 bits and baud rate is 1000, bit rate = 4000 bps

questions.question: Explain Shannon's Theorem and its significance in data communication.
keyPoints: Channel capacity C = B × log₂(1 + S/N)
B = bandwidth, S/N = signal-to-noise ratio
Gives maximum theoretical data rate for noisy channels
Shows relationship between bandwidth, noise, and data rate

questions.question: What are the different transmission modes? Explain parallel and serial transmission.
keyPoints: Serial: one bit at a time over single channel
Parallel: multiple bits simultaneously over multiple channels
Synchronous: timed by shared clock
Asynchronous: uses start/stop bits for synchronization

questions.question: Define the following: (i) Amplitude (ii) Frequency (iii) Phase (iv) Period
keyPoints: Amplitude: Maximum displacement from equilibrium
Frequency: Number of cycles per second (Hz)
Phase: Position within one cycle
Period: Time for one complete cycle (T = 1/f)

questions.question: A telephone channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB. Calculate the maximum data rate that can be transmitted using: (a) Shannon's Theorem (b) Nyquist Theorem assuming 4 signal levels.
keyPoints: Shannon: Convert SNR dB to ratio: SNR = 10^(30/10) = 1000
Nyquist: Maximum bit rate = 2B × log₂M = 2 × 3000 × log₂(4) = 6000 × 2 = 12 kbps
Practical limit is the lower of both (Shannon gives theoretical maximum)

questions.question: Explain the factors affecting data rate in data communication. How do Nyquist and Shannon theorems help in determining channel capacity?
keyPoints: Factors: Bandwidth, signal levels, noise, channel impairment
Nyquist: For noiseless channel, relates bandwidth and signal levels to data rate
Shannon: For noisy channel, relates bandwidth and SNR to maximum capacity
Both formulas provide upper bounds on data transmission

questions.question: Discuss the various types of noise encountered in data communication and their effects on signal transmission.
keyPoints: Thermal noise: caused by electron motion, present in all electronic devices
Impulse noise: sudden short-duration spikes, often from external sources
Crosstalk: interference from adjacent channels
Intermodulation noise: from non-linear components creating new frequencies
All noise reduces SNR and therefore reduces channel capacity

questions.question: Explain different signal encoding techniques used in data communication. Compare NRZ and Manchester encoding.
keyPoints: NRZ-L: voltage level represents bit value throughout bit interval
NRZ-I: voltage change represents 1, no change represents 0
Manchester: transition in middle of bit - 0 to 1 for 0, 1 to 0 for 1
Manchester provides clock synchronization but requires twice the bandwidth
NRZ has DC balancing problems, Manchester solves this with transitions
